# Inference for regression parameters and predictions assuming $\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 \mI)$ {#ols_inference}

[({{< meta texts.cb.initials >}} §11.3; {{< meta texts.wms.initials >}} §11.4–11.7, 11.12–11.13)]{.small}

So far, we have only used the assumptions that 

  - $\mX$ is fixed and of full column rank
  - $\Ex(\vveps)=\vzero$ and $\cov(\vveps)=\sigma^2 \mI$

Going forward, we assume that $\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 \mI)$.  This allows us to

- Make probabilistic statements about the estimators $\hvbeta$ and $\hmu(\vx)$
- Construct confidence intervals and bands for $\vbeta$, $\mu(\vx)$, and $\sigma^2$
- Construct prediction intervals and bands for $Y \mid \vx$
- Perform hypothesis tests about $\vbeta$ and $\mu(\vx)$
- Assess model fit and compare models

## Confidence regions for $\vbeta$ and $\mu(\vx)$ assuming $\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 \mI)$

Since a [linear combination of normal random variables is normal]{.alert}, the following [data-based]{.alert} quantities are all normally distributed:

- $\vY = \mX \vbeta + \vveps \sim \Norm(\mX\vbeta,\sigma^2 \mI)$

- $\hvbeta = (\mX^\top \mX)^{-1} \mX^\top \vY = (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top (\mX \vbeta + \vveps) = \vbeta + (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vveps \sim \Norm\!\left(\vbeta,\sigma^2 (\mX^\top \mX)^{-1}\right)$

- $\hmu(\vx) = \vx^\top \hvbeta  = \mu(\vx) + \vx^\top (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vveps \sim \Norm\!\left(\mu(\vx),\sigma^2 \vx^\top (\mX^\top \mX)^{-1} \vx\right)$

- $\hvveps = (\mI-\mP)\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 (\mI-\mP))$

```{python}
#| label: lr-imports-and-data
#| echo: false

# code to display the regression example
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import sys
from pathlib import Path

from matplotlib.patches import Ellipse, Rectangle
from scipy.stats import t, f

from classlib.plots.colors import (
    TOL_BRIGHT,
    curve_color,
    accent_color,
    neutral_color,
    set_tol_bright_cycle,
)

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path("../classlib/classlib/model_fit").resolve()))
from regression_helpers import generate_quadratic_regression_example

plt.rcParams.update({

    # --- FONT FAMILY (serif + LaTeX-style math) ---
    "font.family": "serif",
    "mathtext.fontset": "stix",
    "font.serif": ["STIXGeneral"],

    # --- FONT SIZES (tuned for slides) ---
    "font.size": 18,
    "axes.titlesize": 22,
    "axes.labelsize": 20,
    "xtick.labelsize": 16,
    "ytick.labelsize": 16,
    "legend.fontsize": 22,
    "figure.titlesize": 22,

    # --- LINE / MARKER STYLE ---
    "lines.linewidth": 2.5,
    "lines.markersize": 7,

    # --- AXES STYLE ---
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.titlepad": 12,

    # --- GRID ---
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.3,

    # --- LEGEND ---
    "legend.frameon": False,
})

nlinspc = 400

EX = generate_quadratic_regression_example(
    seed_start=47,
    seed_stop=47,
    beta_true=np.array([0.10, 0.90])
)
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 6.5
#| fig-height: 3.2
x = EX["x"]
y = EX["y"]
X = EX["X"]
beta_true = EX["beta_true"]
beta_hat = EX["beta_hat"]

xg = np.linspace(0, 3, nlinspc)
Xg = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(xg), xg**2])

mu_true_g = Xg @ beta_true
mu_hat_g = Xg @ beta_hat

# True errors and residuals
mu_vals = X @ beta_true
muhat_vals = X @ beta_hat

eps = EX["eps"]
ehat = EX["resid"]

# Pick a nice positive epsilon_i
i = np.argmax(eps)

# Pick a nice negative residual ehat_j
j = np.argmin(ehat)

xi, yi = x[i], y[i]
xj, yj = x[j], y[j]

mu_i = mu_vals[i]
muhat_j = muhat_vals[j]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()
set_tol_bright_cycle(ax)

data_color = TOL_BRIGHT["blue"]
true_color = TOL_BRIGHT["green"]
fit_color = TOL_BRIGHT["red"]
guide_color = neutral_color
annot_color = accent_color

# Data and curves
ax.scatter(x, y, s=28, color=data_color)
ax.plot(
    xg, mu_true_g,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=true_color,
    label=r"$\mu(x) = \beta_1 + \beta_2 x^2$"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, mu_hat_g,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=fit_color,
    label=r"$\widehat{\mu}(x) = \widehat{\beta}_1 + \widehat{\beta}_2 x^2$"
)

# Highlight chosen data points in the same color as the data
ax.scatter([xi], [yi], s=90, color=data_color, zorder=5)
ax.scatter([xj], [yj], s=90, color=data_color, zorder=5)

# Vertical guide lines
ax.axvline(xi, linestyle=":", linewidth=1.5, color=guide_color)
ax.axvline(xj, linestyle=":", linewidth=1.5, color=guide_color)

# epsilon_i arrow
ax.annotate(
    "",
    xy=(xi, yi),
    xytext=(xi, mu_i),
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", lw=2, color=annot_color),
)

ax.text(
    xi + 0.05,
    0.5 * (yi + mu_i),
    rf"$\epsilon_{{{i}}}$",
    va="center",
    color=annot_color,
)

# residual arrow
ax.annotate(
    "",
    xy=(xj, yj),
    xytext=(xj, muhat_j),
    arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", lw=2, color=annot_color),
)

ax.text(
    xj + 0.05,
    0.5 * (yj + muhat_j),
    rf"$\widehat{{\epsilon}}_{{{j}}}$",
    va="center",
    color=annot_color,
)

from classlib.plots.plot_utils import annotate_xaxis_marks

annotate_xaxis_marks(
    ax,
    [xi, xj],
    [rf"$x_{{{i}}}$", rf"$x_{{{j}}}$"],
    colors=[accent_color, accent_color],
)

ax.set_xlabel(r"$x$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$y$")
ax.set_title(
    rf"Data, $\mu(x)$, $\widehat{{\mu}}(x)$, $\epsilon_{{{i}}}$, and $\widehat{{\epsilon}}_{{{j}}}$"
)
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

---

### Confidence ellipsoid for $\vbeta$ assuming $\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 \mI)$

- From the normal distribution of $\hvbeta$, it follows that
$$\frac{
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top
(\mX^\top\mX)
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
}{\sigma^2}
\sim \chi^2_d
$$

- Also, $\hvbeta = \vbeta + (\mX^\top \mX)^{-1}\mX^\top \vveps = \vbeta + (\mX^\top \mX)^{-1}\mX^\top \mP \vveps$ since $\mP \mX =  \mX$, so $\hvbeta$ depends only on $\mP\vveps$

- Moreover, $S^2 = \lVert(\mI - \mP)\vveps\rVert^2/(n-d)$, so $S^2$ depends only on $(\mI-\mP)\vveps$

- Since $\hvbeta$ and $S^2$ depend on orthogonal projections of $\vveps$ onto $\cc(\mX)$ and $\cc(\mX)^\perp$, respectively, and $\vveps$ is Normal, $\hvbeta$ and $S^2$  are independent. 

- Hence, replacing $\sigma^2$ with $S^2$ gives
$$
\frac{
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top(\mX^\top\mX)(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
}{dS^2}
\sim F_{d,n-d}
$$

---

Therefore 

$$
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top
(\mX^\top\mX)
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
\le
d S^2
F_{d,n-d,\alpha}, \quad \text{where } \Prob(F_{d,n-d} > F_{d,n-d,\alpha}) = \alpha
$$

defines a $\class{alert}{(1-\alpha)\text{ confidence ellipsoid for }\vbeta}$ centered at $\hvbeta$

---

### Individual confidence intervals for the $\beta_j$ assuming $\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 \mI)$

::: {.exitem}
<span class="exbullet">$\exstar$</span><span>Show that the variance of $\hbeta_j$ is $\sigma^2\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}$, where $\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}$ denotes the $j$th diagonal entry of $(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}$ </span>
:::

Since $\hvbeta \sim
\Norm\!\left(
\vbeta,\;
\sigma^2(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}
\right)
$, it follows that for each $j=1,\dots,d$,

$$
\hbeta_j \sim \Norm\!\left(
\beta_j,\;
\sigma^2\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}
\right)
$$

where $\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}$ denotes the $j$th diagonal entry of $(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}$. Replacing $\sigma^2$ by $S^2$ gives

\begin{gather*}
\frac{
\hbeta_j-\beta_j
}{
S\sqrt{\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}} 
}
\sim t_{n-d}
\\
\hbeta_j
\pm
t_{n-d,\alpha/2}
\;
S\sqrt{\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}} \;
\text{ is an }\class{alert}{(1-\alpha)\text{ confidence interval for }\beta_j} \text{ for each } j=1,\dots,d
\end{gather*}

---

### Joint vs individual confidence statements

\begin{gather*}
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top
(\mX^\top\mX)
(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
\le
dS^2F_{d,n-d,\alpha} \quad \text{is a joint confidence region \class{alert}{for the whole vector $\vbeta$}}
\\
\hbeta_j
\pm
t_{n-d,\alpha/2}
\;
S\sqrt{\bigl[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\bigr]_{jj}},
\; j=1,\dots,d, \quad \text{are \class{alert}{individual confidence intervals for each $\beta_j$}}
\end{gather*}


```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 5.8
#| fig-height: 4.6

from classlib.plots.colors import TOL_BRIGHT, accent_color
from regression_helpers import confidence_ellipse_shape

beta_hat = EX["beta_hat"]
beta_true = EX["beta_true"]
beta_ci = EX["beta_ci"]
XtX_inv = EX["XtX_inv"]
s2 = EX["s2"]
n = EX["n"]
d = EX["d"]
alpha = EX["alpha"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots()

ellipse_color = TOL_BRIGHT["blue"]
ci_color = accent_color
truth_color = TOL_BRIGHT["green"]

# Joint confidence ellipse
ell = confidence_ellipse_shape(XtX_inv, s2, alpha, n, d)
width = ell["width"]
height = ell["height"]
angle = ell["angle"]

ellipse = Ellipse(
    xy=beta_hat,
    width=width,
    height=height,
    angle=angle,
    fill=False,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=ellipse_color,
)
ax.add_patch(ellipse)

# Marginal confidence-interval cross
ax.hlines(
    beta_hat[1],
    beta_ci[0, 0],
    beta_ci[0, 1],
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=ci_color,
)

ax.vlines(
    beta_hat[0],
    beta_ci[1, 0],
    beta_ci[1, 1],
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=ci_color,
)

# Points
ax.scatter(
    [beta_hat[0]],
    [beta_hat[1]],
    s=90,
    color=ellipse_color,
    zorder=5,
    label=r"$\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}$",
)

ax.scatter(
    [beta_true[0]],
    [beta_true[1]],
    s=110,
    marker="x",
    linewidths=2.5,
    color=truth_color,
    zorder=6,
    label=r"$\boldsymbol{\beta}$",
)

# Labels for marginal intervals
ax.text(
    0.7 * beta_ci[0, 0] + 0.3 * beta_ci[0, 1],
    beta_hat[1],
    r" CI for $\beta_1$",
    color=ci_color,
    va="bottom",
    ha="center",
)

ax.text(
    beta_hat[0],
    0.5 * (beta_ci[1, 0] + beta_ci[1, 1]),
    r" CI for $\beta_2$ ",
    color=ci_color,
    rotation=90,
    va="center",
    ha="left",
)

ax.set_xlabel(r"$\beta_1$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$\beta_2$")
ax.set_title(r"Joint confidence ellipse and individual confidence intervals")

# Tighter limits
pad_x = 0.08
pad_y = 0.05

x_min = min(beta_ci[0, 0], beta_hat[0] - width / 2)
x_max = max(beta_ci[0, 1], beta_hat[0] + width / 2)

y_min = min(beta_ci[1, 0], beta_hat[1] - height / 2)
y_max = max(beta_ci[1, 1], beta_hat[1] + height / 2)

ax.set_xlim(x_min - pad_x, x_max + pad_x)
ax.set_ylim(y_min - pad_y, y_max + pad_y)

ax.set_aspect("equal", adjustable="box")
ax.legend()

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

:::{.key-point}

* Joint confidence for $\vbeta$ is not the same as simultaneous confidence for all $\beta_j$

* The confidence ellipsoid for $\vbeta$ and the individual confidence interval for $\beta_1$ both include $\beta_1 = 0$ so one might question whether there should be a constant term in the model

:::

---

### Caution about many intervals

If we form separate $(1-\alpha)$ confidence intervals for each $\beta_j$,
the probability that \class{alert}{all} of them are simultaneously correct is generally less than $(1-\alpha)$

So:

- individual confidence intervals control each $\beta_j$ separately
- the confidence ellipsoid controls $\vbeta$ jointly


## Confidence interval for $\mu(\vx)$ assuming $\vveps \sim \Norm(\vzero,\sigma^2 \mI)$

Recall $\mu(\vx)=\vx^\top\vbeta$, $\hmu(\vx)= \mu(\vx) + \vx^\top (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vveps \sim \Norm\!\left(\mu(\vx),\sigma^2 \vx^\top (\mX^\top \mX)^{-1} \vx\right)$

Replacing $\sigma^2$ by $S^2$ gives

$$
\frac{
\hmu(\vx)-\mu(\vx)
}{
S
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
}
\sim t_{n-d}
$$



Therefore

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,\alpha/2}
\;
S
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
$$

is a [$(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $\mu(\vx)$]{.alert}


## Prediction interval for $Y\mid \vx$

A _new_ observation, $Y \mid \vx = Y_{\text{new}}=\vx^\top\vbeta+\varepsilon_{\text{new}}$, depends both on 

- the value of $\vx$ and 
- the new error $\varepsilon_{\text{new}}\sim\Norm(0,\sigma^2)$ that is independent of the data used to fit the model

Predicting $Y_{\text{new}}$ by $\hmu(\vx)$ has uncertainty coming both from the uncertainty in the model parameters and the new error term:

\begin{align*}
Y_{\text{new}}-\hmu(\vx) & = \bigl(\vx^\top\vbeta+\varepsilon_{\text{new}}\bigr) - \bigl(\vx^\top\vbeta + \vx^\top (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vveps \bigr) \\
& = \varepsilon_{\text{new}} - \vx^\top (\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\mX^\top\vveps\\
& \sim \Norm\!\left(0,\;
\sigma^2\left(
1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
\right)\right) \qquad \text{since $\varepsilon_{\text{new}}$ is independent of $\vveps$}
\end{align*}

Replacing $\sigma^2$ by $S^2$ gives a $t$-distribution for the standardized prediction error. A [$(1-\alpha)$ prediction interval]{.alert} for $Y_{\text{new}}$ is then given by

$$
Y_{\text{new}}
\in
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,\alpha/2}
S
\sqrt{
1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
}
$$

## Mean vs prediction intervals

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="49%"}

[Confidence]{.alert} interval for mean response, $\mu(\vx)$:

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,\alpha/2}
S
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
$$

:::

::: {.column width="2%"}

:::

::: {.column width="49%"}

[Prediction]{.alert} interval for a new observation, $Y \mid \vx$:

$$
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
t_{n-d,\alpha/2}
S
\sqrt{
1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx
}
$$

:::

:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 4.2

from classlib.plots.colors import TOL_BRIGHT, accent_color, neutral_color
from regression_helpers import mean_and_prediction_bands

x = EX["x"]
y = EX["y"]
beta_true = EX["beta_true"]
beta_hat = EX["beta_hat"]
XtX_inv = EX["XtX_inv"]
s2 = EX["s2"]
n = EX["n"]
d = EX["d"]
alpha = EX["alpha"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4.2))
set_tol_bright_cycle(ax)

data_color = TOL_BRIGHT["blue"]
true_color = TOL_BRIGHT["green"]
fit_color = TOL_BRIGHT["red"]
ci_color = accent_color
pi_color = TOL_BRIGHT["orange"] if "orange" in TOL_BRIGHT else TOL_BRIGHT["red"]

# grid for plotting
xg = np.linspace(0, 3, nlinspc)
Xg = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(xg), xg**2])

mu_true_g = Xg @ beta_true

bands = mean_and_prediction_bands(
    Xg, beta_hat, XtX_inv, s2, alpha, df=n - d
)

mu_hat_g = bands["mu_hat"]
ci_lo = bands["ci"][:, 0]
ci_hi = bands["ci"][:, 1]
pi_lo = bands["pi"][:, 0]
pi_hi = bands["pi"][:, 1]

# Data and curves
ax.scatter(x, y, s=28, color=data_color)

ax.plot(
    xg, mu_true_g,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=true_color,
    label=r"$\mu(x) = \beta_1 + \beta_2 x^2$"
)

ax.plot(
    xg, mu_hat_g,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=fit_color,
    label=r"$\widehat{\mu}(x) = \widehat{\beta}_1 + \widehat{\beta}_2 x^2$"
)

# Confidence interval (dashed)
ax.plot(
    xg, ci_lo,
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2.2,
    color=ci_color,
    label=r"95% CI for $\mu(x)$"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, ci_hi,
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2.2,
    color=ci_color
)

# Prediction interval (dotted)
ax.plot(
    xg, pi_lo,
    linestyle=":",
    linewidth=2.4,
    color=pi_color,
    label=r"95% PI for $Y \mid x$"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, pi_hi,
    linestyle=":",
    linewidth=2.4,
    color=pi_color
)

ax.set_xlabel(r"$x$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$y$")
ax.set_title(r"Fitted mean with 95% pointwise confidence and prediction intervals")

box = ax.get_position()
ax.set_position([box.x0, box.y0, box.width * 0.78, box.height])

ax.legend(loc="center left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 0.5))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```

::: {.key-point}

* Prediction intervals are wider than confidence intervals

* These pointwise intervals exhibit uncertainty at each fixed $\vx$, [not simultaneously]{.alert} for all $\vx$ at once

:::

## Simultaneous confidence and prediction bands

- Pointwise intervals control uncertainty at one fixed $\vx$; "$\forall \vx$" is _outside_ the probability statement

- A [band]{.alert} controls uncertainty [simultaneously]{.alert} for all $\vx$; "$\forall \vx$" is _inside_ the probability statement

\begin{multline*}
\bigl|\hmu(\vx)-\mu(\vx)\bigr|
=
\bigl|\vx^\top(\hvbeta-\vbeta)\bigr| = \bigl|[(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1/2}\vx]^\top  [(\mX^\top\mX)^{1/2} (\hvbeta-\vbeta)]\bigr| \\
\le
\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
\sqrt{(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top(\mX^\top\mX)(\hvbeta-\vbeta)} \quad \text{for all }\vx \qquad \text{by Cauchy--Schwarz}
\end{multline*}

So the confidence ellipse,$(\hvbeta-\vbeta)^\top(\mX^\top\mX)(\hvbeta-\vbeta)
\le dS^2F_{d,n-d,\alpha}$ 
with probability $1-\alpha$, implies the following [$(1-\alpha)$ confidence band]{.alert}:

$$
\mu(\vx)
\in
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
\sqrt{dF_{d,n-d,\alpha}}\;
S\sqrt{\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
\qquad \text{for all }\vx
$$

Similarly, a [$(1-\alpha)$ prediction band]{.alert} is

$$
Y_{\text{new}}
\in
\hmu(\vx)
\pm
\sqrt{dF_{d,n-d,\alpha}}\;
S\sqrt{1+\vx^\top(\mX^\top\mX)^{-1}\vx}
\qquad \text{for all }\vx
$$

---

:::{.key-point}
* Pointwise intervals use the $t$ multiplier because they control one fixed $\vx$

* Simultaneous bands use the larger $\sqrt{dF_{d,n-d,\alpha}}$ multiplier because they must work for all $\vx$ at once
:::

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| fig-width: 11
#| fig-height: 4.4

from classlib.plots.colors import TOL_BRIGHT, accent_color
from regression_helpers import mean_prediction_and_simultaneous_bands

x = EX["x"]
y = EX["y"]
beta_true = EX["beta_true"]
beta_hat = EX["beta_hat"]
XtX_inv = EX["XtX_inv"]
s2 = EX["s2"]
n = EX["n"]
d = EX["d"]
alpha = EX["alpha"]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(11, 4.4))
set_tol_bright_cycle(ax)

data_color = TOL_BRIGHT["blue"]
true_color = TOL_BRIGHT["green"]
fit_color = TOL_BRIGHT["red"]
ci_color = accent_color
pi_color = TOL_BRIGHT["orange"] if "orange" in TOL_BRIGHT else TOL_BRIGHT["red"]

# grid for plotting
xg = np.linspace(0, 3, nlinspc)
Xg = np.column_stack([np.ones_like(xg), xg**2])

mu_true_g = Xg @ beta_true

bands = mean_prediction_and_simultaneous_bands(
    Xg, beta_hat, XtX_inv, s2, alpha, df=n - d
)

mu_hat_g = bands["mu_hat"]

ci_lo = bands["pointwise_ci"][:, 0]
ci_hi = bands["pointwise_ci"][:, 1]

pi_lo = bands["pointwise_pi"][:, 0]
pi_hi = bands["pointwise_pi"][:, 1]

sci_lo = bands["simul_ci"][:, 0]
sci_hi = bands["simul_ci"][:, 1]

spi_lo = bands["simul_pi"][:, 0]
spi_hi = bands["simul_pi"][:, 1]

# data and curves
ax.scatter(x, y, s=28, color=data_color)

ax.plot(
    xg, mu_true_g,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=true_color,
    label=r"$\mu(x) = \beta_1 + \beta_2 x^2$"
)

ax.plot(
    xg, mu_hat_g,
    linewidth=2.5,
    color=fit_color,
    label=r"$\widehat{\mu}(x) = \widehat{\beta}_1 + \widehat{\beta}_2 x^2$"
)

# pointwise confidence interval
ax.plot(
    xg, ci_lo,
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2.0,
    color=ci_color,
    label=r"95% pointwise CI for $\mu(x)$"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, ci_hi,
    linestyle="--",
    linewidth=2.0,
    color=ci_color
)

# simultaneous confidence band
ax.plot(
    xg, sci_lo,
    linestyle="-.",
    linewidth=2.2,
    color=ci_color,
    label=r"95% simultaneous confidence band"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, sci_hi,
    linestyle="-.",
    linewidth=2.2,
    color=ci_color
)

# pointwise prediction interval
ax.plot(
    xg, pi_lo,
    linestyle=":",
    linewidth=2.2,
    color=pi_color,
    label=r"95% pointwise PI for $Y \mid x$"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, pi_hi,
    linestyle=":",
    linewidth=2.2,
    color=pi_color
)

# simultaneous prediction band
ax.plot(
    xg, spi_lo,
    linestyle=(0, (6, 2)),
    linewidth=2.0,
    color=pi_color,
    label=r"95% simultaneous prediction band"
)
ax.plot(
    xg, spi_hi,
    linestyle=(0, (6, 2)),
    linewidth=2.0,
    color=pi_color
)

ax.set_xlabel(r"$x$")
ax.set_ylabel(r"$y$")
ax.set_title(r"Pointwise intervals and simultaneous bands")

box = ax.get_position()
ax.set_position([box.x0, box.y0, box.width * 0.75, box.height])

ax.legend(loc="center left", bbox_to_anchor=(1.02, 0.5))

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
```
::: {.key-point}

Simultaneous bands are wider than pointwise intervals because they must cover
\class{alert}{all} $\vx$ values at once, not just one fixed $\vx$

:::

## Computing regression quantities via QR decomposition

Let $\mX \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ have full column rank, and write its QR decomposition as

```{=html}
<div style="display:flex; justify-content:center; margin-top:1.5rem;">

<svg width="900" height="400" viewBox="0 0 900 400" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">

  <!-- X -->
  <rect x="60" y="40" width="140" height="320"
        fill="#dceaf7" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="130" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="42" font-family="sans-serif">X</text>
  <text x="130" y="350" text-anchor="middle" font-size="28" font-family="serif">
    <tspan font-style="italic">n</tspan>
    <tspan font-style="normal"> × </tspan>
    <tspan font-style="italic">d</tspan>
  </text>

  <!-- equals -->
  <text x="250" y="160" text-anchor="middle" font-size="48" font-family="serif">=</text>

  <!-- Q -->
  <rect x="300" y="40" width="140" height="320"
        fill="#dceaf7" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"/>
  <text x="370" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="42" font-family="sans-serif">Q</text>
  <text x="370" y="350" text-anchor="middle" font-size="28" font-family="serif">
    <tspan font-style="italic">n</tspan>
    <tspan font-style="normal"> × </tspan>
    <tspan font-style="italic">d</tspan>
  </text>

<!-- R background -->
<rect x="490" y="40" width="140" height="140"
      fill="#f7f7f7"/>

<!-- upper triangle -->
<polygon points="490,40 630,40 630,180"
         fill="#dceaf7"/>

<!-- border (draw LAST) -->
<rect x="490" y="40" width="140" height="140"
      fill="none" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"/>

  <!-- diagonal -->
  <line x1="490" y1="40" x2="630" y2="180" stroke="black" stroke-width="3"/>

  <!-- R label -->
  <text x="560" y="80" text-anchor="middle" font-size="42" font-family="sans-serif">R</text>
  <text x="525" y="145" text-anchor="middle" font-size="42" font-family="sans-serif">0</text>
  <text x="560" y="170" text-anchor="middle" font-size="28" font-family="serif">
    <tspan font-style="italic">d</tspan>
    <tspan font-style="normal"> × </tspan>
    <tspan font-style="italic">d</tspan>
  </text>

</svg>

</div>
```

- $\mQ \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times d}$ has orthonormal columns ($\mQ^\top \mQ = \mI_d$)
- $\mR \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times d}$ is upper triangular

---


Then it follows that

- [Least squares estimate]{.alert}: $\hvbeta = \mR^{-1} (\mQ^\top \vY)$

- [Projection matrix]{.alert}: $\mP = \mQ \mQ^\top$

- [Fitted values]{.alert} $\hvY = \mQ (\mQ^\top \vY)$

- [Residuals]{.alert}: $\hvveps = \vY  - \hvY$

- [Covariance of $\hvbeta$]{.alert}: $\cov(\hvbeta) = \sigma^2 \mR^{-1} \mR^{-\top}$

  - [Variance of $\hbeta_j$]{.alert}: $\sigma^2 \bigl[(\mR^{-1} \mR^{-\top})\bigr]_{jj}$, where $\bigl[(\mR^{-1} \mR^{-\top})\bigr]_{jj}$ denotes the $j$th diagonal entry of $\mR^{-1} \mR^{-\top}$

- [Variance of $\hmu(\vx)$]{.alert}: $\var(\hmu(\vx)) = \sigma^2 \lVert (\mR^{-\top} \vx)\rVert^2$
